naturalMeaning: A weekly open-air market that sets up on a fixed day in a fixed spot and packs up again by early afternoon — see the culture notes on how this actually works.

examples.text: Vou na feira todo sábado de manhã.
examples.translation: I go to the market every Saturday morning.

naturalMeaning: The full, formal name for what everyone just calls 'a feira' — 'livre' (free/open) distinguishes it from an indoor supermarket.

examples.text: A feira livre do bairro é na terça e no sábado.
examples.translation: The neighborhood street market is on Tuesdays and Saturdays.

naturalMeaning: The person who owns and runs a stall — same word for a man or woman, only the article changes: o feirante / a feirante.

examples.text: O feirante me deu uma dica de qual manga escolher.
examples.translation: The vendor gave me a tip on which mango to pick.

examples.text: Aquela barraca de frutas ali é a melhor da feira.
examples.translation: That fruit stall over there is the best one at the market.

examples.text: Ele colocou as maçãs na balança.
examples.translation: He put the apples on the scale.

naturalMeaning: Bringing your own sacola is common and appreciated — many feirantes don't hand out free bags for everything the way a supermarket does.

examples.text: Trouxe minha própria sacola pra feira.
examples.translation: I brought my own bag for the market.

naturalMeaning: Used for a bag of one specific thing, like 'um saco de batata' (a bag of potatoes), rather than the general tote you carry everything in.

examples.text: Me dá um saco de cebola, por favor.
examples.translation: Give me a bag of onions, please.

examples.text: Um quilo de tomate, por favor.
examples.translation: A kilo of tomatoes, please.

naturalMeaning: Vendors are used to this instead of you having to say '500 gramas' — it's the everyday way to ask for a small amount.

examples.text: Só meio quilo de morango, tá bom.
examples.translation: Just half a kilo of strawberries is fine.

examples.text: Uma dúzia de ovos caipira, por favor.
examples.translation: A dozen free-range eggs, please.

examples.text: Um maço de couve, por favor.
examples.translation: A bunch of collard greens, please.

naturalMeaning: Usually used in the plural, 'trocados'. Carrying some is almost expected at a feira — see the culture notes on cash norms.

examples.text: Deixa eu ver se tenho trocado.
examples.translation: Let me see if I have some change.

examples.text: Só aceito dinheiro nessa barraca.
examples.translation: I only take cash at this stall.

naturalMeaning: More stalls take cartão and Pix now, but not all of them — always ask before assuming.

examples.text: Vocês passam cartão?
examples.translation: Do you take card?

naturalMeaning: Free, instant transfers via QR code or phone number — now the single most common way to pay a feirante who doesn't take cards.

examples.text: Posso fazer um Pix pra você?
examples.translation: Can I send you a Pix payment?

examples.text: Não tenho troco pra cem reais.
examples.translation: I don't have change for a hundred reais.

naturalMeaning: Covers leafy greens like alface, couve, and rúcula — as opposed to 'legume' for non-leafy vegetables like batata and cenoura.
Covers things like batata, cenoura, and abobrinha, distinct from the leafy 'verdura'.

examples.text: Essas bananas estão bem maduras.
examples.translation: These bananas are nice and ripe.

naturalMeaning: Also means 'sleeve' (of a shirt) — context makes it obvious which one is meant.

examples.text: Essa manga tá no ponto.
examples.translation: This mango is perfectly ripe.

naturalMeaning: Also slang for a headache of a situation — 'descascar um abacaxi' means to deal with a thorny problem.
The base of 'couve com linguiça' and the classic side to feijoada.
The diminutive of 'fresco' — the word every feirante calls out about their produce, and the reason early-morning shopping matters. See the culture notes on timing.

examples.text: Tá tudo fresquinho, colhido hoje de manhã!
examples.translation: It's all nice and fresh, picked this very morning!

naturalMeaning: Feminine form is 'madura'; agrees with the fruit — uma manga madura.
'Está no ponto' is what a vendor tells you about a fruit that's ready to eat right now — not too green, not overripe.

examples.text: Esse abacaxi tá no ponto, pode levar.
examples.translation: This pineapple is perfectly ripe, you can take it.

naturalMeaning: Diminutive of 'verde' applied to fruit — 'tá meio verdinha ainda' means it needs a day or two more before eating.

examples.text: Pode pesar essas maçãs pra mim?
examples.translation: Can you weigh these apples for me?

examples.text: Posso escolher as peças eu mesmo?
examples.translation: Can I pick out the pieces myself?

examples.text: Pode embrulhar o peixe separado?
examples.translation: Can you wrap the fish separately?

naturalMeaning: The generic verb for haggling anywhere in Brazil — at a feira it stays mild and friendly, see the culture notes on how it actually works.

examples.text: Aqui na feira todo mundo pechincha um pouquinho.
examples.translation: Here at the market everyone haggles a little bit.

naturalMeaning: Feminine is 'freguesa'. A feirante's fregueses are recognized by name and often get first pick or a little extra thrown in — see the culture notes on building that relationship.

examples.text: Ela já é freguesa antiga dessa barraca.
examples.translation: She's already a long-time regular at that stall.

translation: Can you give me a little discount?
naturalMeaning: Literally 'make a little price?' — the standard, friendly way to ask for a small discount; the diminutive keeps the tone light rather than pushy.

lemma: Leva mais barato se eu levar mais?
translation: Is it cheaper if I take more?
naturalMeaning: Haggling at a feira is mostly about volume discounts, not bargaining down the price of a single item — this is the natural way to ask.
A good opener with a vendor you don't know yet — invites a recommendation instead of guessing blind.
Said half-jokingly when you hand the choice over to the feirante — a small ritual of trust that often gets you extra-generous or extra-fresh picks.

translation: Do you have change for a twenty?
Can you put it in a bag?

naturalMeaning: The natural feira goodbye between a vendor and a regular customer — warm and casual, and it assumes you'll be back on the same day next week.
